coredata.dc:title: Integrating carbon quantum dots with oxygen vacancy modified nickel-based metal organic frameworks for photocatalytic CO2 reduction to CH4 with approximately 100 % selectivity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ziqiong
Wang, Yan
Li, Wanting
Liu, Siyu
Zhang, Ling
Yang, Jiani
Feng, Caixia
Chong, Ruifeng
Zhou, Yanmei
coredata.dc:description: Solar-light-driven reduction of CO2 into renewable fuels has great potential in the production of sustainable energy, addressing the energy crisis and environmental problems simultaneously. However, it is a significant challenge to achieve high selectivity for the conversion of CO2 into CH4, which is a type of fuel with a high calorific value. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were integrated with an oxygen vacancy modified nickel-based metal organic frameworks (NiMOFs) to form the CQDs-X/NiMOFV series, which exhibited superior performance for CO2 photoreduction into CH4 compared with pure NiMOFs in the presence of hole scavengers under visible light irradiation. The highest yielding rate of CH4 (1 mmol g−1 h−1) and selectivity (97.58 %) were obtained using a CQDs-25/NiMOFV catalyst. Most importantly, in diluted CO2 atmosphere, the yield of CH4 was almost unchanged and the selectivity of CH4 over CQDs-25/NiMOFV was higher than that in pure CO2. The superior performance of CQDs-25/NiMOFV may be attributed to the following two factors: (i) both CQDs and oxygen vacancies facilitate the transmission of electrons to promote the eight-electron reaction producing CH4, and (ii) oxygen vacancies can act as the electron trap to capture the photogenerated electrons to react with adsorbed CO2 on Ni2+. This study offers a valuable strategy for designing efficient photocatalysts to convert CO2 into CH4 with superior selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic CO2 reduction
High selectivity
Oxygen vacancy
Carbon quantum dots

Optical absorbance and band gap structure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of NiMOF(a), NiMOFV (b), CQDs-10/NiMOFV (c), CQDs-25/NiMOFV (d), CQDs-50/NiMOFV (e) and CQDs-75/NiMOFV (f).
TEM images of NiMOFV (a), CQDs (b), CQDs-10/NiMOFV (c), CQDs-25/NiMOFV (d, e, f), CQDs-50/NiMOFV (g), CQDs-75/NiMOFV (h) and NiMOF(i).
XRD patterns of NiMOF, NiMOFV and CQDs-X/NiMOFV series catalysts.
FTIR spectra of NiMOF, NiMOFV and CQDs-X/NiMOFV series catalysts.
The N 1s (a), Ni 2p (b) and C 1s (d) spectra of NiMOF, NiMOFV, CQDs-25/NiMOFV and CQDs-75/NiMOFV as well as the O 1s (c) spectra of NiMOFV, CQDs-25/NiMOFV and CQDs-75/NiMOFV.

(A) UV–Vis absorbance spectra of various CQDs solutions diluted by 50-fold (a), 100-fold (b) and 200-fold (c). the insets are the photographs of various CQDs solutions diluted by 50-fold (a), 100-fold (b) and 200-fold (c) under day light (top) and 365 UV irradiation (bottom). (B) UV–Vis DRS spectra of NiMOFV, CQDs-10/NiMOFV, CQDs-25/NiMOFV, CQDs-50/NiMOFV and CQDs-75/NiMOFV. (C) (αhυ) 1/2 vs. photon energy curves of different catalysts. (D) The Mott-Schottky plots of typical sample CQDs-25/NiMOFV measured at frequencies of 1500 Hz and 2000 Hz. (E) The valence-band XPS (VB-XPS) spectrua of NiMOFV, CQDs-25/NiMOFV and CQDs-75/NiMOFV.

(a) The generation rates of CO and CH4 under various conditions over CQDs-25/NiMOFV sample, (b) H2 evolution, (c) CO evolution, (d) CH4 evolution, (f) (CO+CH4) evolution and (g) residue CO2 ratio vs. irradiation time as well as (e) the selectivity of CO and CH4 over different catalysts under visible light irradiation (cut-off filter, λ > 420 nm, light intensity is 25.26 mW/cm2); Inset in Fig. 8e.: The ESR intensity (IESR) and CH4 selectivity (SCH4) of all catalysts.

(a) CO evolution, (b) CH4 evolution and (d) the residual CO2 vs. irradiation time as well as (c) the selectivity of CO and CH4 over CQDs-25/NiMOFV catalyst in different CO2 concentration under visible light irradiation.

CO evolution amount (a) and CH4 evolution amount (b) vs. irradiation time over CQDs-25/NiMOFV catalyst under different wavelength. The dependence of carbon-containing gas generation rate and the light intensity on the wavelength of exciting light.

The PL spectra (a), photocurrent response (b) and EIS Nyquist plots (c) of NiMOFV, CQDs-10/NiMOFV, CQDs-25/NiMOFV, CQDs-50/NiMOFV and CQDs-75/NiMOFV catalysts. The inset of (b) shows the comparison of photocurrent response between NiMOF and NiMOFV; The inset of (c) shows the comparison of EIS Nyquist plots between NiMOF and NiMOFV; The data of both insets as well as the data of (b) and (c) are measured at different time periods.
The ESR spectra of (a) DMPO-O2 − adduct, (b) and (c) TEMPO-h+ adduct for NiMOF, NiMOFV and CQDs-25/NiMOFV catalysts in dark and under visible light irradiation.
(a) In situ DRIFTS analysis of CO2 reduction under visible light irradiation over CQDs-25/NiMOFV as well as (b) the possible catalytic mechanism over NiMOF and CQDs-X/NiMOFV catalysts.
Preparation of NiMOF(a), NiMOFV (b), CQDs (c) and CQDs-X/NiMOFV (d) series catalysts via the facile hydrothermal method.
Textural characteristics of NiMOFV and CQDs-X/NiMOFV series catalysts.
The atomic percent of O, C and Ni elements in NiMOFV, CQDs-25/NiMOFV and CQDs-75/NiMOFV.

head.ce:title: Integrating carbon quantum dots with oxygen vacancy modified nickel-based metal organic frameworks for photocatalytic CO2 reduction to CH4 with approximately 100 % selectivity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Joint Research Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control Materials of Henan, College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Joint Research Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control Materials of Henan
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Joint Research Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control Materials of Henan, College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The as-synthesized CQDs-X/NiMOFV heterojunctions exhibit superior performance for CO2 photoreduction into CH4 than normal NiMOF and novel NiMOFV catalyst. The highest yielding rate of CH4 and selectivity is obtained over CQDs-25/NiMOFV catalyst with a value of 1 mmol g−1 h−1 and 97.58 % selectivity, respectively. The superior performance of CQDs-25/NiMOFV may be attributed to the following two factors: (i) Both CQDs and oxygen vacancies facilitate the transmission of electrons to promote the eight-electrons-reaction producing CH4, (ii) oxygen vacancies can act as the electron trap to capture the photogenerated electrons to react with adsorbed CO2 on Ni2+. Moreover, in diluted CO2 (50 %, 20 %, and 10 %) atmosphere, the yield of CH4 is almost unchanged and the selectivity of CH4 over CQDs-25/NiMOFV is apparently higher than that of in pure CO2.

Solar-light-driven reduction of CO2 into renewable fuels has great potential in the production of sustainable energy, addressing the energy crisis and environmental problems simultaneously. However, it is a significant challenge to achieve high selectivity for the conversion of CO2 into CH4, which is a type of fuel with a high calorific value. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were integrated with an oxygen vacancy modified nickel-based metal organic frameworks (NiMOFs) to form the CQDs-X/NiMOFV series, which exhibited superior performance for CO2 photoreduction into CH4 compared with pure NiMOFs in the presence of hole scavengers under visible light irradiation. The highest yielding rate of CH4 (1 mmol g−1 h−1) and selectivity (97.58 %) were obtained using a CQDs-25/NiMOFV catalyst. Most importantly, in diluted CO2 atmosphere, the yield of CH4 was almost unchanged and the selectivity of CH4 over CQDs-25/NiMOFV was higher than that in pure CO2. The superior performance of CQDs-25/NiMOFV may be attributed to the following two factors: (i) both CQDs and oxygen vacancies facilitate the transmission of electrons to promote the eight-electron reaction producing CH4, and (ii) oxygen vacancies can act as the electron trap to capture the photogenerated electrons to react with adsorbed CO2 on Ni2+. This study offers a valuable strategy for designing efficient photocatalysts to convert CO2 into CH4 with superior selectivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Artificial photosynthesis is an effective means of converting CO2 to value-added chemicals using solar energy, accomplishing a sustainable carbon cycle and alleviating the energy crisis. However, because of the high dissociation energy of CO of ca. 750kJ∙mol−1 [1,2], CO2 is thermodynamically stable and difficult to reduce. Among the diverse reduction products, CH4 as a high energy density fuel has attracted considerable attention. Nevertheless, the conversion of CO2 to CH4, which requires eight electrons (CO 2 + 8H + + 8e − → CH4 + 2H2O), is kinetically difficult to occur [3,4]. Although some catalysts have been reported to catalyze the conversion of CO2 into CH4, they still suffer from low conversion efficiency and poor selectivity. It remains a huge challenge to increase the conversion efficiency and selectivity for CO2-to-CH4 conversion [5].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a stable crystalline materials with a three-dimensional network structure through the coordination of organic ligands with metal ions [6–8]. Because of their high surface area, porous structure, and wide absorption range, MOF materials have been widely used in photocatalytic CO2 reduction [9,10]. Chen et al. found that Ni-MOF materials (Ni-abtc) can photocatalytically reduce CO2 to CO with a high generation rate of 1.913 × 104 μmol g−1h−1 and selectivity of 91.4 % but without the production of CH4 [11]. Xu et al. constructed CdS/Ni-MOF heterostructure photocatalysts, in which the optimal catalyst 20 %-CdS/Ni-MOF showed the best CO yield of 7.6 μmol g−1 and CH4 yield of approximately 0.45 μmol g−1 [12]. The conversion efficiency of CO2 to CH4 is still very low. Dai and his coworkers integrated Cu2O@Cu-MOF core–shell cocatalysts with TiO2 to form (366.0 μmol g−1h−1) and hydrocarbon selectivity (CH4 + C2H6, 95.5 %) [13]. A heterogeneous system integrating cocatalyst with MOFs will simultaneously improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers and lower reaction barriers, thereby achieving efficient photocatalytic CO2 reduction.

In the present work, NH4Cl powder was added to the reaction system involving NiCl2·6H2O, terephthalic acid, water, ethanol, and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), during the synthesis of NiMOF catalysts. At a reaction temperature of 140 ℃, ammonium chloride volatilizes completely and even partly decomposes to form reductive ammonia gas, which can promote the formation of oxygen vacancies in NiMOF lattices, acquiring novel nickel metal–organic frameworks with large amounts of oxygen vacancies, denoted as NiMOFV. Finally, novel NiMOFV was integrated with strong conductive carbon quantum dots to synthesize the heterogeneous catalyst CQDs/NiMOFV. Oxygen vacancies can trap the photogenerated electrons to enhance the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges. CQDs can promote electron transfer and greatly improve the selectivity of CO2-to-CH4. The optimal photocatalyst CQDs-25/NiMOFV exhibited the highest CH4 yield (6.1 mmol∙g−1) and the best CH4 selectivity (97.58 %). Such yield and selectivity are almost higher than all reported catalysts for the reduction of CO2 to CH4.

All chemicals were analytical grade and were used without any further purification. Nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), triethanolamine (TEA), citric acid monohydrate (CA), thiourea (CH4N2S), urea (CH4NO2), absolute ethanol (EtOH), and acetonitrile (CH3CN) were purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Company. Tris (2,2-bipyridyl) ruthenium (II) chloride hexahydrate (Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O) was purchased from Shanghai Bide Pharmatech Company Limited. DMF was purchased from Tianjin Deen Chemical Reagent Company. Terephthalic acid (BDC, 99.0 %) was purchased from Aladdin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of normal NiMOF and NiMOFV catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: NiMOFV catalysts were prepared using a facile hydrothermal method. As shown in Scheme 1 b, 0.1783 g of NiCl2·6H2O (0.75 mmol) and 0.1246 g of terephthalic acid (BDC, 0.75 mmol) were added to a mixture solution containing water (2 mL), ethanol (2 mL), and dimethylformamide (DMF, 32 mL) under ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. Then, 0.18 g of NH4Cl was added to the above solution under 10 min of ultrasonic treatment. The resultant solution was moved to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and then maintained at 140 °C for 24 h. Finally, the resulting products were collected via centrifugation, washed with absolute ethanol and H2O, and dried in a vacuum oven at 60°C overnight to obtain a green NiMOFV catalyst. A normal NiMOF catalyst was prepared using the same method without the addition of NH4Cl (Scheme 1a).

A CQD solution was prepared using a simple hydrothermal method described elsewhere [14]. As shown in Scheme 1c, 0.7685 g of citric acid monohydrate (CA, 4 mmol) and 0.9134 g of thiourea (CH4N2S, 12 mmol) were dissolved in 40 mL deionized water, ultrasonicated for 10 min, and stirred for 20 min to obtain a transparent solution. The resultant solution was then moved to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 180°C for 6 h. Finally, the supernatant containing CQD was collected by centrifugation at 8000 rev/min for 10 min.

As illustrated in Scheme 1d, 0.1783 g of NiCl2·6H2O (0.75 mmol) and 0.1246 g of terephthalic acid (BDC, 0.75 mmol) were added to a mixture solution containing water (2 mL), ethanol (2 mL), and dimethylformamide (DMF, 32 mL) under ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. Then 0.18 g of NH4Cl was dissolved to the above solution under ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. Subsequently, a certain volume of CQD solution (10, 25, 50, 75) was added to above solution, and the solution was stirred for 20 min. The resultant solution was moved to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 140°C for 24 h. Finally, the products were filtered and washed with water and ethanol and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 ℃ overnight to obtain CQDs-X/NiMOFV series of catalysts (CQDs-10/NiMOFV, CQDs-25/NiMOFV, CQDs-50/NiMOFV, and CQDs-75/ NiMOFV).

The morphologies of NiMOF, NiMOFV, and CQDs-X/NiMOFV series catalysts were investigated using a scanning electron microscope and transmission electron microscope. As shown in Fig. 1 , NiMOF displayed a regular spherical stacking structure of nanosheets. Because of the expansion of ammonia during the heating process, NiMOFV and CQDs-X/NiMOFV series exhibited an elongated irregular spherical structure, and the pore size and volume were inflated by the gas. The flower-like microspheres consisted of nanosheets with a thickness of approximately 100 nm, suggesting that NiMOF, NiMOFV, and CQDs-X/NiMOFV series have large specific surface areas and many active sites for CO2 reduction. Fig. 2 a, and i show the TEM images of NiMOFV and NiMOF, which further confirm that the structures of NiMOF and NiMOFV are composed of stacked sheets, and NiMOFV is fluffier than NiMOF. Fig. 2b shows the morphology of the carbon quantum dot (CQD). CQD was spherical and had a uniform size of approximately 4–5 nm. Fig. 2c, e, g, h show the TEM images of CQDs-X/NiMOFV series catalysts. CQDs were successfully fabricated on the surface of NiMOFV, and with an increase in added CQD solution volume during the synthesis process, the number of CQDs on the surface of CQDs-X/NiMOFv increaseed. The HRTEM image of CQDs-25/NiMOFV (Fig. 2f) showed the spacing values of the lattice fringes were 0.18 and 0.17 nm, which corresponded to the (102) and (004) facets of CQDs, respectively. To further clarify the chemical compositions of the samples, the EDS mappings (Fig. S1) of C, Ni, O, and N of the typical sample, CQDs-25/NiMOFV, were obtained. C, Ni, O, and N were distributed evenly in the composite, implying that the excellent CQDs/NiMOFV catalyst was successfully synthesized.

N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were measured to reveal the specific surface area and porous characteristics of the samples. As depicted in Fig. S2, all catalysts displayed type IV isotherms with an H3 hysteresis loop, indicating the nature of the mesoporous structure. Abundant micropores are beneficial to the migration of charge carriers and foster the photocatalytic activity of catalysts. The corresponding porosity properties are listed in Table 1 . The BET surface areas of NiMOFV, CQDs-10/NiMOFV, CQDs-25/NiMOFV, and CQDs-50/NiMOFV were higher than those of pure NiMOF, suggesting that the addition of NH4Cl during the synthesis process can make the stacking structure of catalysts fluffier and increase the specific surface area. For CQDs-75/NiMOFV, excess carbon quantum dots loaded on the NiMOFV may inhibit the formation of pores and decrease the specific surface area. The XRD patterns of the as-prepared catalysts are shown in Fig. 3 . As shown, the characteristic peaks of NiMOF and NiMOFV located at 8.67° (8.45), 14.72°, 15.74°, and 16.91° can be indexed to the (200), (001), (201), and (−201) crystallographic planes of monoclinic NiMOF [14], respectively. It has been found that the XRD patterns of CQDs-X/NiMOFV obtained after loading CQDs onto NiMOFV do not shift significantly. This implies that the effect of CQDs on the phase structure of the NiMOFV sample is negligible.

Spectra obtained using a fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) can provide structural information. As shown in Fig. 4 , NiMOF, NiMOFV and CQDs/NiMOFV series had almost identical absorption peaks. The twin peaks at 3593 and 3530 cm−1 corresponded to the NH stretching vibration on primary amines connected to the benzene ring. With the added CQD solution increase, the intensity of the NH absorption peaks increased gradually. This indicated that the formation of the NH bond may be related to multiple materials involved in the reaction, including the DMF solvent with strong alkalinity, thermally unstable ammonium chloride, and thiourea with –NH2 in the CQD solution. The strong peaks at 1588 and 1373 cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the OC-O groups [14]. The weak peak at 1508 cm−1 corresponded to the vibration of the CC groups in the benzene ring [15,16]. The peaks at 826 and 746 cm−1 were from the OH and CH vibration in the H2BDC ligand [17,18], and those at 550 cm−1 were from the stretching vibration of Ni-O [19]. Finally, the inorganic structure of the absorption peak of CQD was not observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyses of defect state and surface composition
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows the electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of NiMOF, NiMOFV, and CQDs-X/NiMOFV series catalysts measured at room temperature in the dark. The Lorentzian line centered at approximately g = 2.0 confirmed the existence of oxygen vacancies [20,21]. As shown in Fig. 5a, the ESR intensity of NiMOFV is much stronger than that of NiMOF, suggesting that the addition of ammonium chloride during the synthesis process of NiMOFV was beneficial to the formation of oxygen vacancy. From NiMOFV to CQDs-25/NiMOFV, with an increase in the CQD content, the ESR signal of oxygen vacancies was first enhanced and then weakened. This may be due to the reducing ammonia gas released from the decomposition of unreacted thiourea in the added CQD solutions resulting in more oxygen vacancy production. However, during the synthesis process of NiMOFV, very many CQD solutions may form carbon atoms to fill oxygen vacancies, leading to a decrease in oxygen vacancy. The ESR intensities of CQDs-50/NiMOFV and CQDs-75/NiMOFV were less than that of NiMOFV. The oxygen vacancies in CQDs-X/NiMOFV can act as the electron trap to capture the photogenerated electrons to react with adsorbed CO2 on Ni2+. Thus, the oxygen vacancies would favor the electron transmission and the photogenerated carrier separation [22–25]. The CO2 adsorption ability of NiMOF、NiMOFV and CQDs-25/NiMOFV catalysts was examined twice by CO2 adsorption performance (Fig. S3). It was found that, the CO2 adsorption ability of NiMOF with less oxygen vacancy was apparently stronger than that of NiMOFV and CQDs-25/NiMOFV, suggesting that the enhanced CO2 conversion to CH4 efficiency of NiMOFV and CQDs-25/NiMOFV could not be attributed to the CO2 adsorption capacity, maybe attributed to the transmission ability of photogenerated electrons.

The spectra of NiMOF, NiMOFV, CQDs-25/NiMOFV, and CQDs-75/NiMOFV obtained using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were tested to investigate surface composition and bonding characteristics. The N 1s spectra of the four samples showed the weak peaks at 401.1 eV (Fig. 6 a), which were presumed to be NH bands [26], consistent with the results of FTIR. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Ni 2p (Fig. 6b) in all samples showed two peaks located at 856.2 and 873.8 eV, along with two satellite peaks at 861.8 and 880.1 eV, which confirmed the existence of Ni2+ in the NiMOF, NiMOFV, and CQDs-X/NiMOFV structures [27]. The symmetrical O 1s peak (Fig. 6c) of NiMOFV centered at 531.8 eV was assigned to the crystal lattice oxygen [28], and the peaks of O 1s in CQDs-25/NiMOFV and CQDs-75/NiMOFV were both negatively shifted to 531.6 and 531.7 eV, indicating that the interaction between NiMOFV and CQDs existed and led to the change in electron density around the lattice oxygen atom in CQDs-X/NiMOFV. As shown in Fig. 6d, the C 1s XPS spectra of NiMOF, NiMOFV, CQDs-25/NiMOFV, and CQDs-75/NiMOFV consisted of two peaks located at 284.8 and 288.6 eV, which were ascribed to CC/CC and CO bonds [29], respectively. Table 2 shows the atom percent of O, Ni, and C in NiMOFV, CQDs-25/NiMOFV, and CQDs-75/NiMOFV. With an increase in the volume of the added CQD solution during the synthesis process from 0 (NiMOFV) to 75 μL (CQDs-75/NiMOFV), the atomic percent of O and Ni decreased and that of C element increased gradually. This indicates that the carbon dots are successfully constructed on the surface of the NiMOF material in CQDs-X/NiMOFV series catalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical absorbance and band gap structure
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 a shows the UV–Vis spectra and photographs of the CQD solution diluted by 50-, 100-, and 200-fold. In the inset, the diluted CQD solution displayed pale yellow luminescence under daylight and bright blue luminescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm, which is the typical characteristic of carbon quantum dots. Moreover, in the UV–Vis spectra, the CQD solution with different concentrations showed a typical absorbance peak centered at 331 nm, which was assigned to the n–π* transition of the CO bond. This further proves that the CQDs were successfully synthesized [17,30].

The optical absorption of NiMOFV and CQDs-X/NiMOFV composites was measured using UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (Fig. 7b). All samples exhibited strong absorption in the visible light region. In particular, in the range of 550–800 nm, the intensity of the absorption peak was consistent with that of the ESR peak, indicating that the visible light absorption ability of the catalyst was effectively affected by the oxygen vacancy in the NiMOF structure. According to the relation between (αhʋ)1/2 and photon energy [31], the Tauc plot (Fig. 7c) revealed that the band gaps of NiMOFV and CQDs-X/NiMOFV series were almost equal, with a value of 2.03 eV. This shows that the oxygen vacancy can effectively promote the visible light absorption ability of catalysts. As shown in Fig. 7d, the Mott-Schottky curves of CQDs-25/NiMOFV (at frequencies of 1500 and 2000 Hz) had a positive slope, suggesting that it is an n-type semiconductor, of which the Fermi level (Ef) is equal to the flat band potential, which was estimated to be −0.49 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl electrode). Considering that E Ag/AgCl was 0.2 V, the Ef of CQDs-25/NiMOFV vs. NHE was determined to be −0.29 eV [32]. Furthermore, XPS valence band spectroscopy (Fig. 7e) demonstrated that the relative potential between the Fermi level and the VB of CQDs-25/NiMOFV was 1.77 eV [33]. Therefore, the VB position of CQDs-25/NiMOFV was determined to be 1.48 eV. Combined with the value of Eg of 2.03 eV obtained from the Tauc plot (Fig. 7c), the CB of CQDs-25/NiMOFV was calculated to be −0.55 eV, more negative than that of E CO2/CH4 (−0.24 V vs. NHE) and E CO2/CO (−0.53 V vs. NHE) [34,35]. This indicates that CO2 could be reduced by the photogenerated electrons in the CB of CQDs-25/NiMOFV to generate CH4 and CO.

In a vapor–liquid-solid reaction system, the photoreduction of CO2 over NiMOF, NiMOFV, and CQDs-X/NiMOFV series catalysts in CH3CN/H2O solution under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm) was performed. CH3CN was added to improve the solubility of CO2 in water. TEOA was used as a hole scavenger and [Ru (bpy)3]2+ acted as the photosensitizer. The experimental setup is shown in Fig. S4. To determine the role of each substance in the photocatalytic system, various control experiments were conducted under different conditions. As shown in Fig. 8 a, over typical catalyst CQDs-25/NiMOFV, without a photosentizier ([Ru (bpy)3]2+), hole scavenger (TEOA), or light, no CO and CH4 were generated. This indicated that CQDs-25/NiMOFV alone cannot trigger the catalytic reaction, and each substance plays an indispensable role in CO2 photoreduction. Once CO2 was replaced by N2, no CO and CH4 were produced yet, which excluded the possibility of carbon-containing products coming from CQDs on the CQDs-25/NiMOFV catalysts and organic additives such as TEOA and [Ru (bpy)3]2+.

The main gas photoreduction product of CO2 on the NiMOF catalyst was CO (Fig. S5a), and almost no CH4 and H2 were produced. The main gas photocatalytic products on NiMOFV and CQDs-X/NiMOFV catalysts were CO and CH4 (Fig. S5c and e). During the illumination process, water was involved in the reaction to be reduced to hydrogen gas (Fig. S5d and f), and the photogenerated holes reacted with water or triethanolamine to produce oxygen (Fig. S5d and f) and some complex oxidation products (Fig. S6 and S7). Fig. 8b and c show the dependence of CO and CH4 production on the illumination time. Compared with CO and CH4, H2 yield over all was very small, far less than 0.1 mmol·g−1·h−1 (Fig. 8d). As shown in Fig. 8b, overall catalysts, CO yield exhibited a similar trend. With an increase in irradiation time, the output of CO reached the maximum value quickly and then decreased gradually. Fig. 8c shows the relationship between CH4 yield and irradiation time over all catalysts. With the prolongation of irradiation time, the production of CH4 increased gradually. This indicates that CO2 is first reduced to CO and then further converted to CH4 under visible-light irradiation. In addition, among all catalysts, CO yield over pure NiMOF catalyst was highest with a value of 8.0 mmol·g−1, and the generation of CH4 was lowest with a value of 0.07 mmol·g−1 in the sixth hour. After introducing large amounts of oxygen vacancies to NiMOFV, the formation of CO decreased from 8.0 to 2.0 mmol·g−1, and CH4 yield increased from 0.07 to 1.9 mmol·g−1 after 6 h of reaction. Most importantly, after loading CQDs on the surface of NiMOFV, all CQDs-X/NiMOFV catalysts exhibited superior catalytic activity than NiMOFV in the conversion to CH4, and the order of CH4 production was CQDs-25/NiMOFV>CQDs-10/NiMOFV>CQDs-50/NiMOFV>CQDs-75/NiMOFV. CQDs-25/NiMOFV displayed a maximum production (6.0 mmol·g−1) of CH4 and a minimum production (0.24 mmol·g−1) of CO after a 6 h reaction. This further confirmed that CO is an intermediate product of CO2 to CH4 conversion. Fig. 8e shows the selectivity of CO and CH4 produced using different catalysts. The selectivity of CH4 over all CQDs-X/NiMOFV catalysts was higher than that over pure NiMOF and NiMOFV. This may be due to the strong conductivity of CQDs, which can facilitate the migration of photogenerated electrons and thus promote the generation of CH4 by the eight-electron reaction. It is worth highlighting that the CQDs-25/NiMOFV catalyst exhibited the highest yield and the best selectivity of CH4 with values of 6.1 mmol.g−1 within 6 h and 97.58 %, respectively. The selectivity of CH4 production by the photoreduction of CO2 was consistent with the intensity of the EPR signal (Fig. 8e inset), demonstrating that the selectivity of CH4 is proportional to the content of oxygen vacancy content, which can act as electron traps, effectively promoting the photoreduction of CO2 with eight-electrons to produce CH4. In summary, the yield and selectivity of CH4 are closely related to CQDs and oxygen vacancies. Moreover, during the illumination process, the trend of carbon-containing product yielding is consistent with the residual amount of CO2 oppositely (Fig. 8f-g). This further indicated that CO and CH4 are produced from the reduction of CO2.

Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of typical catalyst CQDs-25/NiMOFV in different CO2 concentrations was studied. As shown in Fig. 9 a and b, in diluted CO2 (50 %, 20 %, and 10 %) atmosphere, compared with pure CO2, the evolution amounts of CO sharply decreased and CH4 was almost unchanged. Importantly, as shown in Fig. 9c, in the diluted CO2, the selectivity of CH4 over CQDs-25/NiMOFV was apparently higher than that of pure CO2. This implied that, in the conversion process of CO2 to CO, the concentration of CO2 is a critical factor. Therefore, once the concentration of CO2 is reduced, the amount of CO generated sharply decreases. The decrease in CO2 concentration has a little effect on the CH4 yield, because the key factor of CH4 production may be the number of photogenerated electrons. In addition, the time-dependent curve of residual CO2 in the reaction can indirectly prove that the products CO and CH4 came from CO2 (Fig. 9d).

The activity of the CQDs-25/NiMOFV catalyst for the photoreduction of CO2 was further examined under irradiation from 420 to 580 nm (Fig. 10 ), and the apparent quantum yields at corresponding wavelengths were also calculated (Table S1). Even at a wavelength of 580 nm, a small amount of CO could be produced with 0.0066 % AQYCO (Table S1). This may be due to the narrow band gap of CQDs-25/NiMOFV with approximately 2.03 eV. Conversely, even at a wavelength of 420 nm with higher energy but low intensity, the eight-electron reaction producing CH4 did not happen. This was because the number of photoelectrons produced by the weak light excited was very small to cause an eight-electron reaction to happen.

The stability of the CQDs-25/NiMOFV photocatalyst was also examined using a long time irradiation experiment (Fig. S8a). Even at 10 h of irradiation, the CH4 production still had an upward trend, which demonstrated that the as-synthesized photocatalyst exhibited good stability. The comparison of the XRD patterns of CQDs-25/NiMOFV before and after 10 h of irradiation further confirmed that the tested catalyst exhibited good stability (Fig. S8b).

Fig. 11 a shows the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of NiMOFV and CQDs-X/NiMOFV series loaded with different amounts of carbon quantum dots at an excitation wavelength of 377 nm. The bare NiMOFV catalyst displayed the highest intensity and the peak of CQDs-25/NiMOFV was considerably weaker than that of pure NiMOFV and other CQDs-X/NiMOFV catalysts, indicating that CQDs-X/NiMOFV series possess much higher separation efficiencies than that of pure NiMOFV, especially CQDs-25/NiMOFV with highest efficiency, which may be due to the strong electron-transfer ability of CQDs [32] and electron-capturing ability of oxygen vacancies [36,37]. Similarly, the transient photocurrent results (Fig. 11b) and EIS data (Fig. 11c) further confirmed that carbon quantum dots and oxygen vacancies can effectively promote the electron transport and most generated photoelectrons in CQDs-25/NiMOFV were involved in the eight-electron reaction to form CH4.

Here, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO) spin-trapping ESR techniques were used to detect the production of NiMOF, NiMOFV, and CQDs-25/NiMOFV active compounds. As shown in Fig. 12 a, no ESR signal was observed in the dark, and the characteristic signals of DMPO-∙O2 − with a relative intensity of 1:1:1:1 were identified for all three catalysts after 5 min of irradiation, suggesting that the conduction band values of NiMOF, NiMOFV, and CQDs-25/NiMOFV were more negative than −0.33 V (EO2/.O2-) vs. NHE [38]. Moreover, the DMPO-∙O2 − signal over CQDs-25/NiMOFV was more intense than that of the other two catalysts. In addition, TEMPO is a stable radical, and its characteristic triplet peaks in the dark are very strong. After 5 min of illumination, the intensities of the TEMPO EPR signals over CQDs-25/NiMOFV decreased much larger than those of NiMOF and NiMOFV. The above results indicated that there was more efficient utilization of the photogenerated charge carrier over CQDs-25/NiMOFV.

To further explore the reaction pathways and intermediates involved in the visible-light-induced photoreduction of CO2 by CQDs-25/NiMOFV catalyst, in situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform (in situ DRIFTS) analysis was performed. As shown in Fig. 13 a, when the light was turned on, some peaks appeared at 1560, 1635, and 1700 cm−1 corresponding to COOH* [39–41], which is widely regarded as the intermediate for CO or CH4. In addition, an apparent peak attributed to CO* emerged at 1869 cm−1 [42]. With an increase in irradiation time, the peak at 1869 cm−1 became weaker and the absorption bands at 1090 and 1151 cm−1 became stronger. The two absorption bands at 1090 and 1151 cm−1 are the characteristic bands of CHO* and CH3O* [43,44], which are the essential intermediates in CO2 to CH4 reduction. The change in peaks at 1869, 1090, and 1151 cm−1 during the illumination process, demonstrated that CO* can be converted to CHO*, and further to CH3O*. On the basis of the above analysis of in situ DRIFTS, the reduction of CO2 to CH4 over CQDs-X/NiMOFV catalyst was likely to proceed as follows:
CO2 + H+ + e− → COOH*
COOH*+ H+ + e− → CO*+H2O
CO* + H+ + e− → CHO*
CHO* + H+ + e− →CH2O* CH2O*+ H+ + e− →CH3O*
CH3O*+ 3H+ + 3e− → CH4 + H2O

As shown in Fig. 13b, for pure NiMOF and NiMOFV, the main product of CO2 reduction was CO due to the weak ability to migrate photoelectrons. After loading CQDs on the surface of NiMOFV, the transmission of photogenerated electrons was effectively accelerated. The oxygen vacancies acting as the active sites can effectively capture multiple electrons to react with CO2, yielding CH4. The synergistic effect between CQDs and oxygen vacancies on boosting the separation of photogenerated charge is a critical factor for the efficient conversion of CO2 to CH4 using the CQDs-X/NiMOFV catalyst.

In summary, the addition of ammonium chloride during the synthesis process of NiMOFV is beneficial to the formation of oxygen vacancy, and improves its capturing electrons ability. After constructing the CQDs on the surface of NiMOFV, the yielding rate and selectivity of CH4 for CQDs-X/NiMOFV were largely enhanced. CQDs and oxygen vacancies can effectively elevate the electron transport rate and electron-capturing ability, and can promote the occurrence of the eight-electron reaction, thereby enhancing the selectivity of CO2-to-CH4 conversion. The highest yield and selectivity of CH4 using the optimum catalyst CQDs-25/NiMOFV can reach 6.0 mmol·g−1 and 97.58 %, approximately 3.22 times (1.86 mmol·g−1) and 1.28 times (76.0 %) more than those of pure NiMOFV, respectively. Compared with other catalysts reported in references (Table S2), the yield and selectivity of CQDs-25/NiMOFV for CO2-to-CH4 conversion were almost the best. More importantly, in the diluted CO2 (50 %, 20 %, and 10 %) atmosphere, CQDs-25/NiMOFv still exhibited nearly unchanged yield and even higher selectivity for CH4. This study provides valuable guidance for constructing novel catalysts for artificial photosynthesis to produce CH4 efficiently and selectively.

Ziqiong Wang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Resources, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yan Wang: Supervision, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wanting Li: Resources, Investigation. Siyu Liu: Software, Data curation. Ling Zhang: Validation, Formal analysis. Jiani Yang: Software, Methodology. Caixia Feng: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ruifeng Chong: Supervision, Investigation. Yanmei Zhou: Supervision, Conceptualization.
